calendar.matters:
CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Feb-24-2020 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-27-2019 Continued To Mar-11-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Motion For Attorneys Fees And Costs
Off Calendar Dec-23-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Laura R. Grealish, Esq. of MURPHY PEARSON BRADLEY & FEENEY, T:415.788.1900, for defendant/moving party;
No other appearance..

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT ROGER GROSS' Motion For Attorneys Fees And Costs, as follows:

Defendant Roger Gross' motion for attorneys' fees and costs is granted in the amount of $126,319.47 in attorneys' fees and $3,152.32 in costs. The Court declines to consider Plaintiff's late-filed opposition, and will not allow argument on the motion. (Cal. R. Ct. 3.1300(d).)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

Off Calendar Oct-25-2017 Continued To Dec-13-17 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-21-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, James A. Quadra of the law offices of Quadra & Coll LLP (415-426-3502) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, John H. Feeney of the law offices of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney (415-788-1900) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT ROGER GROSS' MOTION TO STRIKE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT (anti-SLAPP motion) is DENIED. The parties agree that the complaint for malicious prosecution implicates petitioning activity and therefore, Mr. Gross meets prong 1 of the anti-SLAPP statute. Even if the court agrees with defendant Gross that the court cannot take judicial notice of the truth of the court's (Judge Robertson) prior findings in the underlying litigation and that the declaration of Dr. Roberts is inadmissible as hearsay that does not meet the former testimony exception, plaintiff still presents sufficient evidence to meet prong 2 of the anti-SLAPP statute. The elements of the malicious prosecution claim are (1) termination in favor of plaintiff; (2) action prosecuted without probable cause; and (3) action initiated with malice. (Franklin Mint Co. v. Manatt, Phelps & Phillips, LLP (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 313, 333.) Here, plaintiff prevailed in the underlying action when the court (Judge Robertson) ruled that the estate planning documents were unenforceable. The court can take judicial notice of the effect of that ruling. Plaintiff presents evidence that the prior action lacked probable cause. A lack of probable cause exists where a litigant "relies upon facts which he has no reasonable cause to believe true, or if he seeks recovery upon a legal theory which is untenable under the facts known to him." (Kleveland v. Siegel & Wolensky, LLP (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 534, 551.) Gilbert Pacaldo declares that he witnessed Mr. Gross put the pen in decedent's hand and sign for her even though she was in a catatonic state. Based on this evidence, Mr. Gross had no reasonable cause to believe that decedent had the capacity to execute the testamentary documents. Plaintiff presents evidence of malice. Malice exists where the person initiating the claim does not believe it to be valid. (Id. at 554.) In this case, Gilbert Pacaldo's declaration indicates that Mr. Gross did not believe the probated documents to be valid. The court rejects Mr. Gross' standing argument. Under Steiner v. Eikerling (1986) 181 Cal.App.3d 639, Mr. Gross' submission of the estate planning documents to the probate court set the machinery of law in motion, and therefore constituted the initiation of legal proceedings, and plaintiff has standing to maintain this action as an aggrieved person. The court rejects the statute of limitations argument. The tolling agreement clearly contains a typographical error, the court resorts to extrinsic evidence to resolve the ambiguity/mistake, and the extrinsic evidence shows that the tolling was to last for 3 months and plaintiff's claim is timely. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: James A. Robertson II; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Viscio, CSR No: 12353, email: mviscio@sftc.org. (D302)
